Figure 17: Wedge charge stance: two matched regiments given the same move order, one wedge-charging — it visibly pulls ahead and reaches the enemy line first.







  
  Your browser can't play this clip --- see the
  repository to run the game.



Figure 18: Knockback focus stance: a unit switches mid-fight and starts hitting softer while visibly shoving the enemy line back harder with every blow.



Relieve a fighting friendly (line relief). Right-click an engaged friendly outside your selection to order a relief: the fresh unit advances into the fight and the tired one peels back toward its own rear to rest. The two regiments pass through each other during the swap — the reliever takes over the melee, and the relieved unit’s retreat is a real move order, so holding Space shows it like any other march. Any fresh order to the reliever cancels the swap where it stands. Relief is how you win a long grind — see Tactics.




  
  Your browser can't play this clip --- see the
  repository to run the game.



Figure 19: Line relief: a fresh unit passes through its tired ally and takes over the fight; the relieved unit falls back to the rear.



Press T to cycle a selected unit’s formation — Tight closes ranks for better missile and charge defence, Loose spreads out, and Square forms the anti-cavalry ring described below. Every unit can cycle through all four; each type just starts at a different one — spearmen deploy Tight (locked shields against a charge), archers deploy Loose (room to fire), and sword-armed infantry and cavalry deploy at the plain Normal combat order.
Square: orbis and schiltron (anti-cavalry rings). Square forms a hollow square: the soldiers face spears and shields outward on every side, so the unit has no weak flank or rear — an attack from any direction lands as if it were frontal, and a cavalry charge into the ring is braced (and backfires) no matter which side it hits. The ring is visible at a glance from the individual soldiers’ own outward-facing marks around the block’s perimeter — no separate overlay drawn on top. The price is mobility (the ring crawls) and offence (it hits softer while hunkered), so square up before the horse arrives and drop back to Normal once the threat is gone. There are two variants of the ring, trading off differently:

Orbis — the last-stand ring. Its offence and charge-bracing sit at the square’s baseline, and it holds its nerve better than any other stance: casualties erode its morale more slowly, so a surrounded unit making a final stand is less likely to break outright. Because it’s a reaction you want to trigger fast, O (for orbis) jumps a unit straight to Square without cycling through Tight and Loose — press it again to drop back to Normal — and the control bar’s Square (Orbis) button does the same with one click.
Schiltron — the cavalry specialist. A denser hedge of levelled spears braces a charge harder than the orbis does, so cavalry that rides into a schiltron comes off worse than riding into an orbis, at the cost of hitting even softer in return. It carries no morale bonus — unlike orbis this isn’t a last-stand stance, just the hard anti-cavalry counter. Shift + O jumps a unit straight to Schiltron — press it again to drop back to Normal — and the control bar’s Schiltron button does the same.

Shielded close-order stances. Two heavier stances build on Tight’s locked-shield density, chosen from the control-bar Formation menu:

Shield Wall — shields locked edge-to-edge in a static line. It sharply cuts frontal missile and melee damage, but only to the front: a shot or blow into the flank or rear bypasses the wall entirely. The wall barely moves, so it is a holding stance — plant it to anchor a line, not to advance.
Testudo — shields locked front, sides, and overhead. It blunts missile fire from every direction, the strongest arrow cover in the game, but the men are packed head-down under their shields and fight weakly, and it is the slowest stance of all. Use it to walk a unit across open ground under heavy archery, then drop back to a fighting formation before you close.

Both stances show their own footprint on the map, so you can read a unit’s stance directly from how the soldiers stand: a shield wall packs into a tight edge-to-edge line, and a testudo packs into a dense, deep block (the turtle) — no separate schematic drawn on top, just the real, physical formation geometry.




  
  Your browser can't play this clip --- see the
  repository to run the game.



Figure 20: Shielded-stance visuals: a shield wall (left) packs into a tight edge-to-edge line, and a testudo (right) packs into a dense block — each stance reads at a glance from the soldiers’ own positions.



Resize the line. A regiment normally forms up at a sensible width, but you can reshape it: press ] to widen the line by one file (shallower, more men fighting at the front) or [ to narrow it (deeper, fewer up front but more staying power). With a single unit selected, two small grips appear on its flanks — drag a grip in or out to set the width directly, with a live preview of the new file count. The width holds as the unit takes casualties, and shows in the info panel.
File-doubling (explicatio and duplicatio). Where [ / ] nudge the width one file at a time, these two drill maneuvers reshape it by a whole factor at once. Press B for explicatio: each file splits and the rear half steps out to the side, so the frontage doubles and the depth halves — a column unfolds into a broad line that brings more men to the front, good for overlapping a flank. Press N for duplicatio: alternate files tuck in behind their neighbours, so the frontage halves and the depth doubles — a wide line folds into a deep, narrow column, good for punching through or squeezing down a lane. Nobody teleports: the soldiers walk to their new slots, so the reshape takes a moment to settle. Explicatio can’t widen past a single rank, and duplicatio bottoms out at one file.




  
  Your browser can't play this clip --- see the
  repository to run the game.



Figure 21: File-doubling: duplicatio (N) folds the line into a deep column; explicatio (B) unfolds it back into a broad line.



Anchored (asymmetric) explicatio. The plain explicatio above widens symmetrically about the unit’s own centre — both flanks step outward by the same amount. Press Shift+B to widen instead while holding the right flank fixed, or Ctrl+B to hold the left flank fixed: the whole widen lands on the opposite side, so the unit’s position on the field shifts rather than staying centred. Useful when a flank must stay pinned to terrain or a neighbouring unit while the line still needs to broaden.




  
  Your browser can't play this clip --- see the
  repository to run the game.



Figure 22: Anchored explicatio (Shift+B): the right flank stays fixed while the whole widen lands on the left.



Deploy with a drag (form up). With a single unit selected, right-click and drag across open ground to lay out where it should stand: the start of the drag is the unit’s left flank, the end is its right flank. The unit marches over and pivots to face the line — the drag sets its position, width, and facing in one gesture, with a live preview of the front line and a forward arrow. A short right-click (no drag) is still a plain move.
Deploy several units at once. Select multiple units first, then drag the same flank line: it’s split into one contiguous slice per unit, with a small gap between them. The split can hold either depth (front-to-back ranks) or width (frontage) equal across the units, and can measure “equal” either by space (physical world units, accounting for each unit’s own density) or by count (a plain rank/file number, blind to density) — four combinations in all:

Equal depth (space) — the default. Every regiment forms up to the same physical depth: a tightly-packed unit fits more ranks into that depth than a loosely-spread one, so the whole front reads as one uniform wall front-to-back.
Equal depth (count) — every regiment gets the same number of ranks regardless of density, so a loosely-spread unit ends up physically deeper than a tightly-packed one at the same rank count.
Equal width (space) — every unit takes the same physical frontage; a smaller unit just stacks deeper to fit its slice.
Equal width (count) — every unit gets the same file count (soldiers per rank), so physical frontage differs when density does.

For a same-density group (every selected unit in the same stance) the space and count variants of a given dimension produce identical results — the distinction only matters when you drag a mixed group (e.g. a Tight unit alongside a Loose one). Press Y to cycle through the enabled modes and Shift+Y to cycle backward; the choice you make in ☰ Menu → Form-up sets the default each battle starts with, and which modes the Y-key cycles through. By default the units fill the line left-to-right by where they already stand, so each marches to the nearest slice without crossing a neighbour; hold Shift while you drag to place them in selection order instead (the first unit you picked takes the left flank). The preview shows every unit’s slice, file count, and the active split mode before you release.




  
  Your browser can't play this clip --- see the
  repository to run the game.



Figure 23: Three regiments of different size — Spearmen (Tight), Infantry (Normal), Archers (Loose) — box-selected and dragged into a flank line under the default equal-depth (space) split: each forms up to the same physical depth. Tight and Normal share the same spacing, but the loosely-spread Archers split the line differently from both for a genuinely density-driven reason.



Survey all orders (hold Space). Holding Space overlays every unit’s current order on the battlefield: a dashed green line to each move destination, a red line to each attack target, and a teal link for support duties. At the end of each move line, a ghost formation (faint green dots) shows exactly where each individual soldier will stand when the regiment finishes its march — so you can check whether units will fit, overlap, or leave a gap before they arrive. The ghost reads the destination stance’s actual shape, so a unit marching in Square (or Schiltron) previews as the real hollow-square footprint, not the wide line the other formations use.




  
  Your browser can't play this clip --- see the
  repository to run the game.



Figure 24: Move-order ghost preview: a unit forms Square, then marches under the hold-Space orders overlay. The destination ghost shows the real hollow-square footprint, matching the stance it is actually marching into.



Each order line also carries a distance label at its midpoint, in the same metres the scale bar uses — the remaining march for a move (the full route, summed over any waypoints), or the range to the target for an attack or support link. The number counts down as the unit closes. Toggle the labels off in ☰ Menu → Order distance labels (on by default).




  
  Your browser can't play this clip --- see the
  repository to run the game.



Figure 25: Order distance labels: the green move line shows the remaining march in metres, counting down as the regiment closes on its target.

title: How to Play
text:
The battle loop
Here is the cycle in practice, shown with a cavalry charge:




  
  Your browser can't play this clip --- see the
  repository to run the game.



Figure 26: Cavalry charge: two horse units receive an attack order and accelerate to full speed on contact.




Select — left-click a unit, or drag a box to grab several at once. The info panel fills with the selected unit’s stats, including a flavor Commander rank (Centurion, Pilus Prior, Decurion, …) drawn from the real Roman officer hierarchy — cosmetic only, it has no effect on combat.
Order — right-click to send them. Move them onto an enemy’s flank, or right-click the enemy directly to attack. There is a brief reaction delay (~0.5 s, faster for cavalry) before units execute — orders aren’t instant.
Watch the bars — each unit flies a small regimental flag in its team colour and shows a strength bar that drops as it takes casualties. The flag is a click target too: clicking it selects the unit, just like clicking the block. Morale matters as much as numbers (see Tactics).
Navigate terrain — the field has two terrain patches: a dark-green forest (slows movement to 60%) on the left flank, and a tan hill (impassable) on the right. Units route around the hill automatically, whether under your orders or fleeing a rout — and a routing unit boxed in with no way around stands to fight instead. See Tactics → Terrain.
Exploit the break — when a unit routs, it flees (its flag fading) and stops fighting for now. Routs spread to nearby allies, so a single break can cascade. A fugitive that breaks contact can rally and reform back under your control at low morale, so chase down a broken enemy to finish it before it recovers — and give your own routers room to escape and rally.
Win — destroy the entire enemy army (every unit dead, or fled clear of the map) and a Victory overlay appears, with Fight Again to restart. A routing enemy isn’t beaten yet: a “broken” one may yet rally, and even a “shattered” one (its nerve gone for good) still has to escape or be run down before it’s actually off the field.

title: How to Play
text:
Campaign Map
Sparta includes a turn-based campaign layer on top of its tactical battles. Select a scenario from the Main Menu to open the campaign map.
On your turn:

Click one of your provinces (highlighted when you have an army there).
Click an adjacent province to move, or attack an enemy one if you are at war.
Press End Turn when you are done. Each AI faction takes its turn automatically.

Diplomacy: the right-hand panel lists every surviving rival. Click Declare War or Sue for Peace to change your stance. Suing for peace sets a truce that prevents re-declaring war for a few turns.
Rulers and AI personality: each faction is led by a named ruler whose trait shapes its diplomacy. The turn banner at top-left names the current faction’s ruler in parentheses (for example, Rome’s banner reads “Rome (Julius Caesar)”).



Table 2: Ruler traits and the strength ratios at which each declares war or sues for peace.










Trait
War threshold
Peace threshold




Normal
Attacks at 1.5× strength advantage
Sues for peace below 0.6×


Aggressive
Attacks at 1.25× advantage
Fights longer (sues below 0.33×)


Defensive
Needs a 2× advantage to attack
Sues for peace sooner (below 0.8×)






Province counts appear in the standings line below the banner. Own every province to win the campaign.

title: How to Play
text:
Recording & replays
Every battle is recorded automatically — a ● REC indicator shows top-center. When a battle ends it’s saved, and you can:

Watch Replay — re-run the battle you just played (▶ REPLAY). You can still pan and inspect units during playback, but can’t issue new orders.
Load Replay — open any earlier saved battle from a file picker (top-right, also on the end screen).
Fight Again — start a fresh, newly-recorded battle.

See Replays for how the deterministic system works and why the logs are tiny.

Source: README.md in the repository.

title: How Combat Works
text: Battles in Sparta are not resolved by two numbers trading blows. Every soldier fights the individual in front of them, carries their own health, stamina, and footing, and each blow is rolled on its own. The tactics you know — flanking kills faster, never turn your back, spears screen, a braced line stops a charge, a surrounded man tires and falls — are not bonuses bolted on. They emerge from where bodies are and the dice each strike rolls. This page lays out those dice.

title: How Combat Works
text:
What each soldier carries
Every soldier has live state that combat reads and changes moment to moment:

Health — wounds add up here; at zero, the soldier falls.
Stamina — spent by every action; when it runs low, everything gets worse.
Facing — the direction they are turned. You can only defend what you face.
Stance — from at ease, through advancing and sprinting, to braced (set to receive a hit), or knocked prone. Stance sets how hard you hit on the move, how well you hold your ground, and how fast you tire — and changing it takes time, so you must be set before the blow arrives.

and fixed traits from their regiment:

Skill \(s\) — training. Lands blows, parries, beats shields, tires slower.
Armour \(a\) — soaks part of a landed blow, from any angle.
Shield \(b\) — blocks blows, but only ones you face.
Lethality \(\ell\) — how deadly the weapon is.
Reach \(r\) — how far the weapon strikes (a spear out-reaches a sword).
Mass \(m\) — how hard they are to shove or knock down (a horse is heavy).

Two things scale everything a soldier does: being wounded and being winded. A hurt or exhausted soldier hits softer, defends weaker, and is easier to put down — so wounds and fatigue snowball.

title: How Combat Works
text:
One blow, step by step
When a soldier swings at the enemy in front of them, the game resolves a closing speed, a contest, a wound, a stamina cost, and a shove.
The formulas below are sketches: a subscript \(A\) or \(D\) means the attacker’s or defender’s value (so \(a_D\) is the defender’s armour), and a trailing \(\cdots\) hides finer factors kept in the full spec. Each symbol is explained where it first appears.
0. How fast are they closing? The attacker’s speed toward the target — call it the charge \(c\) — is zero standing still and large for a galloping horse. It makes the blow harder to dodge, harder-hitting, and harder-shoving, all at once. Closing speed belongs to both fighters: it counts for whoever strikes during the rush, in either direction, and head-on speeds add. So a spearman who holds his point into a charge spits the rider on its own momentum, and two horsemen charging each other trade blows at combined speed — cavalry into cavalry is the deadliest clash there is.
1. Does it land? (attack vs. defence) The blow is an opposed roll: the attacker’s offence against the defender’s active defence — footwork, parry, shield. Crucially, you can only actively defend what you can see:
\[p_{\mathrm{land}} = L\big(\beta\,(\mathcal{A} - \mathcal{D})\big),
\qquad
\mathcal{D} = \phi\,(s_D + \lambda\,b_D)\cdots\]
Here \(\mathcal{A}\) and \(\mathcal{D}\) are the attacker’s and defender’s scores, \(L\) turns the gap between them into a hit chance (an even gap is even odds), and \(\beta\) sets how sharply skill tells. \(\phi\) is your facing against the incoming blow: full to the front, little to the flank, zero to the back — and \(\lambda\) is how much a shield (\(b_D\)) adds to a defence you can actually bring to bear. A blow to a soldier’s back meets no parry and no shield — only armour. Skill, shields, and footwork all live on the front. Wound the attacker or wind the defender and these scores shift; nothing is ever a guaranteed hit or miss.
2. Does it wound? A blow that lands subtracts from the defender’s health — we don’t flip a kill-or-not coin, we track damage. Its size is the weapon’s lethality, boosted by the charge and blunted by armour:
\[\Delta h = D_0\,\ell_A\,(1 + c)\,(1 - a_D)\cdots\]
Here \(\Delta h\) is the health lost and \(D_0\) a base damage scale. There is no separate “injured” flag: health just drops, and a soldier near death fights weaker, so the next wound comes easier than the last. At zero health they fall. Armour is the only thing that protects a man hit from behind.
3. Everyone tires. Stamina is spent by attacking, by defending (every blow you meet costs you — a charge costs more), and by getting up after being knocked down. It refills only while resting. So a soldier under sustained pressure, or surrounded, burns through stamina, their defence collapses, and then blows land freely. Fatigue, not a hidden timer, is what breaks a line.
4. The shove. Every blow that connects — even a blocked one — shoves the target back, harder from a charge and less against a heavy soldier:
\[J = J_0\,\frac{\ell_A\,(1 + c)}{m_D}\cdots\]
Here \(J\) is the shove (an impulse) and \(J_0\) its base strength. A blocked blow draws no blood but still pushes — which is how a spear wall drives a stalled enemy backward.
Shoves from several attackers in the same moment add up, but the summed speed is capped: a pile-on in a crowded press hurls a man back a few body-lengths at most, never across the field. The uncapped force still decides whether he is knocked down, so a heavy hit fells as hard as ever.

title: How Combat Works
text:
Knocked down — and the domino
A hard enough shove knocks a soldier prone. Bracing and mass make you harder to topple; once down, you defend nothing, can’t strike, and must spend time and stamina scrambling up — which is exactly when the next blow kills you. Prone soldiers appear as dark horizontal marks (or a lying silhouette at high zoom), so a charge that bowls men over is visible at a glance.
A shoved soldier also collides with whoever is behind them. Bracing soaks only so much — but the men behind help. Braced comrades in a tight file back up the man in front, so the front rank resists with the whole column’s footing, not one man’s. A shove smaller than that combined footing dies at the front; a hit stronger than the column can absorb bowls the front man over and the surplus dominoes rearward, fading as it goes:
\[J_{i+1} = \tau\,\big(J_i - \mathrm{capacity}_i\big)_+, \qquad 0 < \tau < 1.\]
Here \(J_i\) is the shove reaching the \(i\)-th man back, \(\mathrm{capacity}_i\) his rank’s combined footing, and \(\tau\) how much of the surplus carries on to the next rank (\((\,\cdot\,)_+\) just means a negative result is zero — a shove fully absorbed stops). So a light shove dies at the first set rank; a heavy charge punches through, toppling rank after rank until it is spent — and the deeper the braced file, the bigger the hit it takes to break through. You are more braced when set (the braced stance), in tight formation, and facing into the blow — the same facing that lets you defend, and the support vanishes the instant the file loosens, opens a gap, or is hit from a side it isn’t facing. A loose, flanked, or sprinting file dominoes and goes down; a deep, set, front-facing shield wall stands.

title: How Combat Works
text:
Why your tactics work
Everything tactical comes out of the rolls above:

Flank and surround. A soldier hemmed in is in reach of more enemies (more blows rolled at them), burns stamina meeting all of them (their defence collapses), and takes hits they can’t face (armour only). Three effects, one cause — not a “rear bonus” number. Get around the flank and the line melts.
Never show your back. Facing gates both your defence and your footing. A soldier fighting to their front is hard to hit and hard to topple; the moment they turn — routing, or caught from behind — defence and footing vanish and they’re run down. That’s why a breaking line dies so fast.
Spears screen. A spear’s reach holds shorter weapons out past their own reach: the spearmen jab and shove while the swordsmen can’t answer. Put a spear line in front of what you want to protect.
Brace for the charge — in time. A charge lights up everything at once — harder to dodge, deadlier, and a shove that bowls men over and lays them out. Into a loose line it’s a massacre. A braced, deep spear line, set and facing, soaks it at the front rank — the ranks behind buttressing the front — stays on its feet, and out-reaches the horse: the charge breaks on the hedge. But setting the brace takes time, so order it before contact (a line caught still at ease is ridden down), keep it deep (a shallow braced line can be punched through), and a heavy enough charge topples men even when braced. Never take a charge loose or in the flank; never send horse into set spears.
Armour and shields buy time. Heavy, shielded troops turn wounds into blocked shoves and glancing hits — they grind slowly and endure. Light troops caught in the press evaporate.
Fight fresh, fight skilled. Skill and stamina help at both ends — landing more, defending more, tiring slower. Rested veterans beat winded levies by more than the headcount suggests, so pace your commitment.







Note



The exact constants (\(\beta\), \(D_0\), \(\tau\), the stamina costs, the knockdown threshold, and the rest) are balance dials we tune against playtests; the shape of the model above is what’s fixed. For the full specification, see the docs/combat-model.md design note in the project repository.

title: Architecture
text: Sparta’s Milestone 1 is one self-contained battle scene wired up in code. The codebase is small and deliberately flat — easy to read end to end.

title: Architecture
text:
Project layout
project.godot          Godot project config (main scene = scenes/Battle.tscn)
scenes/Battle.tscn     Main scene: camera + units container + selection + HUD
scripts/
  Battle.gd            Spawns armies, enemy AI, win/lose check, tick clock + replay orders
  Replay.gd            Deterministic record/playback (autoload): seeded RNG + order log
  Unit.gd              Regiment: stats, movement, melee, flanking, morale, routing
  SelectionManager.gd  Click + drag-box selection, move/attack orders
  CameraController.gd  WASD / edge pan, wheel zoom
  HUD.gd               Hint bar, unit info panel, victory/defeat overlay
assets/                CC0 art goes here (see Assets) --- not required to run






NoteA deliberate deviation from a scene-heavy design



The UI is built in code in HUD.gd instead of separate .tscn files, and units are instantiated in code (Unit.new() in Battle.gd) instead of a Unit.tscn. Fewer scene files to corrupt, functionally equivalent.

title: Architecture
text:
The scripts at a glance



Table 1: Each script and its responsibility.





Script
Responsibility




Battle.gd
Spawns two armies, runs the enemy AI, the fixed-tick clock, applies queued orders, checks win/lose, saves the replay on battle end.


Unit.gd
A regiment: stats and the IDLE→MOVING→FIGHTING→ROUTING/DEAD state machine, movement, melee with flanking, morale/routing, soft separation, and _draw() visuals.


SelectionManager.gd
Left-click + drag-box selection; right-click move/attack order issuing.


CameraController.gd
WASD / arrow / screen-edge pan, mouse-wheel zoom.


HUD.gd
Hint bar, selected-unit info panel, victory/defeat overlay, active pause, ● REC indicator.


Replay.gd
Autoload: the single seeded RNG and the record/playback system (see Replays).

title: Architecture
text:
Design pillar: collision is core
In a large-scale tactical battle, where the bodies are is the game. Sparta treats collision as a first-class system, not polish: units must physically occupy space, press against each other, hold formation, and be blocked by friend and foe. Flanking, screening spearmen, cavalry charges, and chokepoints are only meaningful if units cannot pass through or stack on one another. Collision correctness and performance take priority over new-feature breadth.
Current state — soft separation, plus everything below. Each frame, a unit pushes out of any overlapping unit (live or routing) by half the overlap; the neighbor corrects the other half. The center-to-center floor is a per-type radius (cavalry wider than infantry), not a shared constant, and it’s intentionally soft so regiments still press into melee contact (attack reach > separation floor) instead of bouncing apart. Two engaged enemy lines close until their front ranks meet, and a spearman line holds firm against cavalry — the horse takes the full correction, so it can’t ride through a screen. The logic lives in Unit.gd → _separate(), on top of the SpatialHash.gd neighbor grid. All five roadmap items below have landed.

Collision roadmap (priority order)

✅ No-stack soft separation between live units.
✅ Per-type footprint — cavalry wider than infantry, so charges and screens read correctly. Landed as the per-type separation radii in Unit.gd.
✅ Formation cohesion — a regiment holds a block/line shape while moving, not a blob. Landed as the block/line slot geometry in UnitFormation.gd.
✅ Hard blocking — spearmen screen and physically stop cavalry passage; lines form chokepoints. Where collision and the rock-paper-scissors design meet. Landed as the asymmetric push-share in Unit.gd → _separate(): a spearman takes none of the separation correction against cavalry, so the horse can’t push through.
✅ Scale — replace the O(n²) neighbor scan with a spatial grid (and/or CharacterBody2D / move_and_slide) before unit counts grow past a few dozen. Landed as SpatialHash.gd/SoldierSpatialHash.gd; see docs/individual-collision-design.md for the full individual-collision design.

title: Architecture
section: Scaling to large battles: two simulation tiers
text:
Scaling to large battles: two simulation tiers
A single battle scene can mix two simulation fidelities at once, one per formation:

Close tier — full per-soldier simulation, exactly the individual-body collision/combat model described above. Used for formations currently in or near active combat.
Far tier — an aggregate record (living count, morale, position, facing) evolved by statistical rules instead of per-soldier physics, for formations far from any fight (reserves, a second echelon). A far tier formation costs the same regardless of how many soldiers it represents, so a handful of aggregate reserve corps can stand in for tens of thousands of soldiers without the per-soldier cost.

A formation crosses tiers automatically as it nears or leaves combat — promoting to close tier as an enemy closes to strike range, demoting back to far tier once it disengages and falls back. See docs/large-scale-simulation-design.md for the full design and the phased build-out.




  
  Your browser can't play this clip --- see the
  repository to run the game.



Figure 1: The engaged close-tier front (fully detailed, individually-simulated soldiers) in the foreground, and the far larger aggregate far-tier reserve force revealed as the camera pulls back.

title: Architecture
text:
Determinism
The whole simulation is built to be reproducible — one seeded RNG stream, a fixed 60 Hz physics tick, and orders applied on the next tick so live play and replay share a code path. This is what powers the replay system and the CI demo clips. See Replays for the full story.

title: Architecture
text:
Tuning pointers



Table 2: Common tuning knobs and where each lives.









Want to change…
Look in…




Unit stats / loadout
Battle.gd → _spawn_line() array


Collision spacing / soft-resolve
Unit.gd → _separate() (floor = RADIUS + other.RADIUS); spawn gaps via spacing in _spawn_line()


Movement pace
Battle.gd → SPEED_SCALE constant (lower = slower)


Combat math
Unit.gd → _strike() / take_casualties() / _flank_multiplier()


Active pause
HUD.gd → _toggle_pause() (Space)


Enemy AI
Battle.gd → _run_enemy_ai()







Source: PLAN.md and README.md in the repository.

title: Assets & Licensing
text: The game runs without any downloaded art — units draw as placeholder tokens in Unit.gd. When real medieval art is wanted, Sparta uses genuinely free sources and follows one hard rule about what may be bundled.

title: Assets & Licensing
section: The one rule: can we bundle it?
text:
The one rule: can we bundle it?
Anything committed under assets/ is redistributed to everyone who clones — or forks — the repo. So a source is usable as a bundled file only if its licence permits standalone redistribution of the (possibly modified) file.



Table 1: Licence classes and whether a file may be bundled in the repo.










Meaning




✅ bundle-safe
CC0 / public domain — zero obligations (we credit anyway).


🅰 bundle-safe, with attribution
CC-BY / CC-BY-SA / OGA-BY — commit, but keep the credit; -SA derivatives stay -SA.


⛔ reference-only
NC, ND, or no standalone redistribution (Mixkit, Pixabay, BBC RemArc). Audition only — never commit the file.












ImportantBeing non-commercial doesn’t loosen this — it tightens it



The blocker is redistribution, not commerciality. Because forks inherit the repo (code is MIT), anything carrying NC or ND is out: NC would bar downstream/commercial forks, and ND forbids the routine adapting we do (trim, resample, recolour, loop).
Litmus test: “Could a stranger who forks our repo — even to ship a commercial game — keep this exact file?” CC0 / CC-BY / CC-BY-SA → yes. NC / ND / Mixkit / Pixabay / BBC RemArc → no.








WarningDo not use commercial-game mod assets



They are not public domain — modders retain copyright and many derive from copyrighted base-game art. Shipping them is legally risky. Stick to the CC0 sources below.

How to add art

Download a pack and unzip it.

Sparta currently ships a CC0 sound set sourced from OpenGameArt (sword clash, swishes, thwack, trumpet, fanfare for hit/shoot/rout/death/select/order/victory/ defeat). Per-file sources and licences are recorded in assets/sfx/CREDITS.md.

The project’s code is MIT-licensed. Bundled assets keep their own licences (CC0 / CC-BY), recorded in the receipts. MIT never implies a licence over the third-party art and audio.



Full running catalog (audio + graphics, including reference-only sources) lives in docs/asset-sources.md; the standing art shortlist is in ASSETS.md.

title: Getting Started
text: Sparta is a Godot 4.7 project. It needs no environment variables, no build step, and — crucially — no downloaded art: units draw themselves as colored placeholder tokens, so the battle is playable the moment you press play.

title: Getting Started
section: Just want to play? Download a build
text:
Just want to play? Download a build
Pre-built binaries for Windows, macOS, and Linux are attached to each GitHub Release. Grab the zip for your platform, unzip it, and run — no Godot install needed.
The builds are unsigned, so the first launch needs one extra step:

macOS (universal — Apple Silicon and Intel): right-click Sparta.app → Open, then confirm. (Or clear the quarantine flag: xattr -dr com.apple.quarantine Sparta.app.)
Windows: if SmartScreen warns, choose More info → Run anyway.
Linux: mark it executable if needed (chmod +x sparta.x86_64) and run it.

To build from source or work on the game instead, follow the developer setup below.

title: Getting Started
text:
1. Install Godot 4.7 (Standard build)
Download Godot 4.7.x — Standard build (GDScript, not the .NET/C# build) from the official downloads page.






Important



Use the Standard build, not .NET. Sparta is pure GDScript; the .NET build is for C# projects and is not what this project targets.

title: Getting Started
text:
2. Import the project

Open Godot.
Click Import.
Select this repository’s project.godot.

title: Getting Started
text:
3. Play
Press F5 (Play). The battle starts immediately:

Two armies spawn — blue at the top (yours), red at the bottom (the enemy).
Units are colored tokens on a green field. No art download is required.

That’s it — you’re in a battle. Head to How to Play for the controls, or Tactics That Matter to start winning.

title: Getting Started
text:
Running headlessly (optional)
You don’t need a display to validate the project — handy for CI or a cloud editor. From the project root:
# Catch parse/load errors without opening the editor:
godot --headless --path . --quit
To actually play, though, you need the desktop editor with a display — a headless environment has no window to render the battle into.

Installing Godot in a headless Linux environment
Most Linux CI (and remote/cloud editors) can run Godot headlessly once it’s installed. Add this to a setup script (matches the project’s Godot version):
wget -q https://github.com/godotengine/godot/releases/download/4.7-stable/Godot_v4.7-stable_linux.x86_64.zip -O /tmp/godot.zip
mkdir -p "$HOME/.local/bin/"
unzip -q /tmp/godot.zip -d "$HOME/.local/bin/"
mv "$HOME/.local/bin/Godot_v4.7-stable_linux.x86_64" "$HOME/.local/bin/godot"
chmod +x "$HOME/.local/bin/godot"
rm /tmp/godot.zip
export PATH="$HOME/.local/bin:$PATH"






Note



If the engine version changes, update both the download URL and the binary filename in the snippet above.

title: Getting Started
text:
Tests
The project has a GUT test suite (vendored at run time in CI). See test/ in the repository for how it’s wired.

Source: README.md in the repository.

title: Sparta
text:
A prototype that fuses dynastic grand strategy with real-time tactical battles — built in Godot 4.7 with GDScript.

Get started → How to play View on GitHub

title: Sparta
text:
What is Sparta?
Sparta is a solo prototype with a deliberate strategy: build the hardest, most differentiating piece first — a real-time tactical battle — as a self-contained vertical slice, get it playable, then grow outward into a campaign.
This repository currently contains Milestone 1: a single, self-contained tactical battle. You command the blue army; defeat the red army on a top-down field where where the bodies are is the whole game.
It runs with zero downloaded art — units render as colored placeholder tokens — so you can clone, open in Godot, press F5, and fight immediately.

title: Sparta
text:
What makes a battle interesting


⚔️ Collision is the core
Units physically occupy space, press against each other, and block friend and foe. Flanking, screening spearmen, cavalry charges, and chokepoints only matter because units can’t pass through or stack on one another.


🛡️ Flanking & morale
Hit a unit from the side (×1.5) or rear (×2) for far more damage and morale loss. Units that lose enough soldiers or take flank hits rout — and routs spread to nearby allies.


🐎 Rock-paper-scissors
Cavalry are fast with a charge bonus, but spearmen blunt that charge. Use cavalry to flank, spears to screen. Read more in Tactics.


🎬 Deterministic replays
Every battle records as a tiny seed-plus-orders log — the same approach many strategy games use. Re-watch any battle, or re-run it to debug. See Replays.

title: Sparta
text:
Jump in

Getting Started — install Godot, import the project, press play.
How to Play — controls, the objective, and the battle loop.
Tactics That Matter — flanking, routing, and the unit triangle.
Replays — how the deterministic record/playback system works.
Architecture — the scripts, scenes, and the collision design pillar.
Roadmap — milestones and the prioritized issue backlog.







Note



This site is generated documentation. The source of truth lives in the Sparta repository — README.md, PLAN.md, REPLAY.md, and ASSETS.md.

title: Roadmap
text: The strategy is a vertical slice: build the hardest, most-differentiating piece (a tactical battle) first, make it fun, then grow outward into a campaign.

title: Roadmap
text:
Milestones



Table 1: The three milestones and their status.










Milestone
Scope
Status




M1
One playable, self-contained tactical battle.
✅ Scaffolded & playable


M2
A dynastic campaign map — clickable provinces, characters/realms, turn-based diplomacy & war. Battles auto-resolved at first.
🚧 In progress — the Gallic War map, data-driven campaigns, and diplomacy (with AI agency) have landed; characters/dynasty and the saga layer remain


M3
Integration — an army battle on the campaign map launches into the M1 battle scene and returns a result (winner, casualties). Where the two genres meet.
🚧 In progress — the battle hand-off and result-applied-to-map round trip have landed; AI-vs-AI/AI-vs-player battle launches and richer army composition remain






The battle scene was kept self-contained specifically to make the M3 hand-off clean.

title: Roadmap
text:
Prioritized backlog
The foundation, the collision pillar (per-type footprint, pathfinding, formation cohesion, hard blocking), and the shared collision-exemption primitive (friendly pass-through, line relief, unit merging) all shipped — see PLAN.md’s design-pillars section for how each landed. With that early backlog cleared, the issue tracker itself is the live source of truth for what’s prioritized now: browse open issues on Lacaedemon/sparta with P0–P3 labels, or the two dominant current threads below.
Two threads dominate the current P2 backlog, both feeding the M2/M3 milestones above:

Campaign layer — phase 1 through phase 7 build out map/time, army composition, battle-resolution modes, real-geography ingestion, logistics, sagas, and dynasty in sequence.
Battle AI — phase 2 through phase 5 build subcommanders, a general layer with doctrine and reserves, player delegation to AI subcommanders, and fog-of-war-aware perception.

Independent of those two threads, P2/P3 also carries near-term fixes and design work (formation/spawn edge cases, the soldier weapon/shield type model, a maneuver-queue refactor) — the tracker’s labels and open-issue list are more current than any snapshot repeated here.

Source: PLAN.md in the repository. The issue tracker is the live source of truth for status.
